draft.introduction: Pliny the Elder’s Natural History Book 2 covers cosmology, astronomy, and meteorology—an encyclopedic attempt to catalog the physical universe. It represents the Roman scientific worldview at its most comprehensive.

translation.text: The world and this—whatever else we call the sky whose vaulted roof encloses the universe—is fitly believed to be a deity, eternal, immense, neither created nor subject to destruction. To seek what is outside it is no concern of man, nor can the human mind comprehend it. It is sacred, eternal, immense, all in all, indeed itself the all.
translation.original_text: Mundum et hoc quodcumque nomine alio caelum appellare libuit cuius circumflexu degunt cuncta, numen esse credi par est

translator_notes.note: Pliny’s identification of the cosmos itself as divine (numen) reflects Stoic pantheism, where nature and God are coextensive. This cosmological piety—awe before the natural world—represents an alternative to both Epicurean atheism and traditional polytheism.
